questions.question: Explain the difference between Separate Chaining and Open Addressing. Also mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each method.
keyPoints: Separate Chaining: stores collisions in linked lists at each bucket
Open Addressing: finds alternative empty slots within the table
Separate Chaining advantage: simple to implement, handles load factor > 1
Open Addressing advantage: better cache performance, no pointers needed
Separate Chaining disadvantage: extra memory for pointers, worst-case O(n)
Open Addressing disadvantage: clustering problems, table must be sized larger than elements

questions.question: What is primary clustering in hash tables? How does Quadratic Probing help in reducing this problem?
keyPoints: Primary clustering: long contiguous blocks form in the table
Elements tend to fill these blocks, causing longer probe sequences
Quadratic Probing uses quadratic increments (i²) instead of linear increments
This spreads out the probing, reducing tendency to form large blocks
However, Quadratic Probing does not completely eliminate clustering

questions.question: Define load factor in hash tables. How does it affect the performance of hash table operations?
keyPoints: Load factor α = n/m, where n = elements, m = table size
For Separate Chaining: higher α means longer chains, search becomes O(1+α)
For Open Addressing: higher α means more collisions and longer probe sequences
When α > 0.7 for open addressing, performance degrades significantly
Rehashing is done when α exceeds threshold to restore performance

questions.question: Explain Double Hashing with an example. Why is it considered better than Linear and Quadratic Probing?
keyPoints: Double Hashing: h(k,i) = (h1(k) + i × h2(k)) mod m
Uses two hash functions: primary h1(k) and secondary h2(k)
Step size varies with each key, eliminating secondary clustering
Example: if h1(k)=7, h2(k)=3, table size=11, probes go to 7, 10, 2, 5...
Better than Linear: no primary clustering
Better than Quadratic: no secondary clustering
Worst-case search is O(1/(1-α)) for open addressing

questions.question: What is rehashing? When and why is it performed in hash tables?
keyPoints: Rehashing: creating a new larger hash table and re-inserting all elements
Performed when load factor exceeds a threshold (typically 0.7-0.75)
Reasons: maintain O(1) average operations, prevent performance degradation
New table size is usually double or 2× prime number of old size
All old hash values become invalid, need recomputation
Trade-off: one-time O(n) cost but restores performance

questions.question: Discuss the problem of deletion in open addressing. How are tombstones used to solve this problem?
keyPoints: Simple deletion creates gaps that break probe sequences
Searching after deletion may fail to find elements in valid positions
Tombstones: special markers placed in deleted slots
Allow continued probing past deleted positions
Eventually table may have many tombstones, affecting performance
Periodic cleaning or rehashing can remove tombstones

questions.question: Consider inserting the keys: 35, 10, 42, 12, 27 into a hash table of size 11 using: (a) Linear Probing (b) Quadratic Probing. Show step-by-step insertion and final table state for each method. Also explain the probe sequences used in each case.
keyPoints: Hash function: h(k) = k mod 11
Linear Probing: probes at positions (h+i) mod 11
Insert 35: h(35)=2, slot 2 empty → insert at 2
Insert 10: h(10)=10, slot 10 empty → insert at 10
Insert 42: h(42)=9, slot 9 empty → insert at 9
Insert 12: h(12)=1, slot 1 empty → insert at 1
Insert 27: h(27)=5, slot 5 empty → insert at 5
Quadratic Probing: probes at positions (h+i²) mod 11
Same insertions with quadratic increments: 35→2, 10→10, 42→9, 12→1, 27→5
Compare probe sequences and clustering behavior
Show how each method handles collisions differently

questions.question: Write pseudocode for insertion and search operations in a hash table using Separate Chaining. Analyze the time complexity of both operations in terms of load factor α.
keyPoints: Insert: compute hash, traverse/add to linked list at bucket
Search: compute hash, traverse linked list to find key
Average case insert: O(1 + α) time
Average case search: O(1 + α) time
Worst case: O(n) when all keys collide to one bucket
With good hash function and load factor < 1, average is O(1)
Deletion same complexity as search

questions.question: Explain the three main open addressing techniques: Linear Probing, Quadratic Probing, and Double Hashing. Compare them in terms of: (i) Probe sequence formula (ii) Clustering problems (iii) Cache performance (iv) Guarantee of finding empty slot.
keyPoints: Linear Probing: P(k,i) = (h(k) + i) mod m - has primary clustering
Quadratic Probing: P(k,i) = (h(k) + i²) mod m - has secondary clustering
Double Hashing: P(k,i) = (h1(k) + i × h2(k)) mod m - eliminates clustering
Linear Probing has best cache performance (contiguous access)
Double Hashing has best distribution but slightly slower due to two hashes
Linear Probing guarantees finding empty slot if table not full
Quadratic Probing only guarantees with certain table sizes and α < 0.5
Double Hashing also guarantees if h2(k) is relatively prime to table size

questions.question: A hash table of size 13 uses open addressing with linear probing. Show the final table after inserting keys: 6, 25, 18, 27, 59, 72, 34 in order. Then perform deletion of key 25 and subsequent search for key 72. Explain how deletion affects the hash table and the search operation.
keyPoints: Hash function: h(k) = k mod 13
Insert sequence and final table: 6→6, 25→12, 18→5, 27→1, 59→7, 72→7→8, 34→8→9
Delete 25: position 12 becomes tombstone
Search 72: computes hash=7, checks 7(empty), 8(found), 9(empty)... but tombstone at 12 must be skipped properly
Tombstone allows search to continue past deleted position
Explain how probe sequences must handle tombstones differently
Deletion does not physically remove elements, marks as deleted for probing purposes
